briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 25, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Record-breaking Heatwave Strikes Europe and Deepening Climate Crisis
sections.content: Recently, Europe has been suffering from unprecedented heatwaves, experiencing health emergencies and significant economic losses. The UK and Switzerland recorded their highest June temperatures ever, heatwave-related deaths are increasing across Europe, and hospitals have declared emergencies due to air conditioning failures and other issues [34, 38, 52, 54, 59]. In particular, the German economy has incurred billions of euros in losses due to the heatwave, and residents on the Caribbean island of Saint Lucia have faced difficulties due to intermittent water supply shortages for several months, illustrating the direct impacts of climate change appearing in various parts of the world [34, 60]. Domestically, the increasing weather risks associated with the expansion of renewable energy have put grid flexibility to the test, and the rejection of climate change plans in Texas has led to a crisis in Corpus Christi, further highlighting the importance of climate action [23, 33].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Spreading Domestic Efforts for Carbon Neutrality and Climate Crisis Response
sections.content: Domestically, diverse efforts are actively underway to transition towards a carbon-neutral society. Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) and the National Police Agency have signed a business agreement to strengthen public sector cooperation for energy conservation and carbon neutrality [10, 11, 13, 14, 16, 19]. The Federation of Korean Industries (FKI) emphasized that carbon neutrality should be approached as a growth strategy rather than a regulation, expressing anticipation for the Carbon Neutrality Industry Act [6, 18]. In local communities, educational and participatory activities are vibrant, with Busan Offshore Wind Power conducting special lectures for elementary school students [2], Pohang City fostering youth climate leaders [3], and the Hwaseong City Institute Carbon Neutrality Support Center recruiting citizen-participatory projects [8]. Furthermore, Jeju City and Hadong County are providing incentives through the Carbon Neutrality Point System to encourage citizens' energy-saving practices [9, 15]. Asan City is reviewing its climate crisis response by strengthening disaster response capabilities and creating carbon-neutral landmarks [5, 17]. These efforts, along with news such as a KBS reporter winning the Climate Change Journalist Award [1] and the successful completion of a regional carbon spatial map report [7], demonstrate the expanding social awareness and practical implementation of climate change response in Korea.

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Facing Challenges
sections.content: Globally, the transition to renewable energy is accelerating, with active investments being made both domestically and internationally. The National Growth Fund has invested 370 billion Korean Won in renewable energy infrastructure, including wind power generation [21, 25]. Hana Bank is also focusing its investments on long-term infrastructure sectors such as renewable energy and AI data centers [24]. Korea is actively pursuing efforts to become one of the top 10 renewable energy powerhouses globally by 2030 [22]. China has also announced plans to establish a new energy system by 2030 [26]. However, the expansion of renewable energy faces challenges such as grid saturation and increased weather risks, and with 650 million people worldwide lacking access to electricity, achieving the universal energy access goal by 2030 is expected to be difficult [20, 23, 30]. Despite these challenges, positive changes are also being observed, such as electric vehicle sales surpassing gasoline car sales for the first time in the UK [63], and renewable energy growth continuing in the US despite federal policy uncertainties [64].

sections.title: 🌍 Global Climate Action and Pressure for Systemic Change
sections.content: Global society's awareness and response to climate change are unfolding in complex ways across legal, financial, and scientific dimensions. The Paris court ruled that TotalEnergies must disclose climate risks and establish a resolution plan, setting an important precedent for corporate responsibility [37, 55]. An analysis of approximately 3,600 climate-related lawsuits filed since 2015 also revealed a report indicating that the proliferation of data centers and AI is becoming a major target for environmental litigation worldwide [56]. Meanwhile, alongside criticism regarding how banks profit by financing industries that exacerbate climate change [36], warnings are also being raised about ineffective 'solutions' to the climate crisis [31]. Climate change poses a direct threat to ecosystems, such as bees [42], and demands new scientific understanding, such as the centuries-long response of the ocean carbon pump [41]. Amidst this, the misconceptions among Americans concerned about climate change [32] and the demands from Americans affected by climate change for answers from their representatives [48] underscore the importance of enhancing public understanding and policy responses. The necessity of seeking innovative solutions, including the utilization of AI [35] and the role of universities [39], is also being highlighted.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, alongside the ongoing comprehensive efforts and challenges to overcome it. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: UK and Switzerland record hottest ever June day as health emergencies surge in Europe
articles.description: Temperatures linked to third child’s death in France, where three-quarters of country is under extreme heat alertEurope live – latest updatesThe UK and Switzerland both recorded the hottest-ever June temperatures on Thursday, while brutally hot conditions supercharged by the climate crisis were linked to the death of a third toddler in France and a sharp rise in medical emergencies across Europe.The UK’s new provisional high of 36.4C (97.5F), recorded in Yeovilton, Somerset, surpassed Wednesday’

summary.phenomenon: On Thursday, record-high June temperatures were recorded in the UK and Switzerland, with a provisional high of 36.4C (97.5F) registered in Yeovilton, Somerset, UK. In France, three-quarters of the country was under an extreme heat alert, and the death of a third toddler and a sharp rise in medical emergencies across Europe were linked to the heatwave.
summary.cause: These brutally hot conditions were further intensified by the climate crisis.

articles.title: Tarmac playgrounds and windows that don’t open: why hot spells turn our schools into heat traps | Harry Paticas
articles.description: Our schools are a dated mix of single glazing, dodgy pipes and atriums like Kew hothouses. They urgently need retrofitting for a changing climateThis week’s soaring summer temperatures have put a spotlight on our schools and their ability to cope, with one in Hertfordshire telling me that it recorded temperatures of more than 40C. So why are our schools struggling?Modern schools often have too much glass, and not enough shading or ventilation to keep out the sun’s heat. During the 1950s, the foc

summary.phenomenon: This week's rising summer temperatures have highlighted schools' ability to cope with heatwaves, with one school in Hertfordshire reporting temperatures over 40C. Modern schools tend to trap heat due to outdated structures such as single glazing, inadequate piping, greenhouse-like atriums, excessive use of glass, and insufficient shading and ventilation.
summary.outlook: Urgent retrofitting of school facilities is required to prepare for a changing climate.

summary.phenomenon: Despite the rainy season beginning in May, the Caribbean island of St. Lucia has been experiencing intermittent water supply shortages for months, making normal hygiene and food preparation difficult. Residents are relying on water tanks, rainwater harvesting, and purchasing bottled water.
summary.cause: Despite millions of dollars in investment, crumbling infrastructure and erratic rainfall are pushing St. Lucia into a water scarcity crisis.
